title: Section 2: The Spinal Cord and Reflex Actions
content: In this section: how the spinal cord processes signals, the reflex arc pathway, and the three types of reflex actions.
bullets: The spinal cord as part of the CNS
The reflex arc — step by step
image: Diagram: cross-section of spinal cord with sensory and motor neurones entering and leaving, brain connected above

title: The Spinal Cord
content: The spinal cord is part of the central nervous system (CNS) and runs from the brain down through the vertebral column.
bullets: Carries impulses to and from brain
image: Diagram: human body outline with brain and spinal cord highlighted as the CNS

title: What Is a Reflex Action?
content: A reflex action is a rapid, automatic response to a stimulus that does not require conscious thought.
image: Illustration: hand pulling back from a hot surface — showing speed of reflex response

title: The Reflex Arc
content: The reflex arc is the pathway an impulse follows from receptor to effector during a reflex action.
bullets: Sensory neurone carries impulse to CNS
Motor neurone carries impulse to effector
image: Labelled diagram: full reflex arc — receptor, sensory neurone, relay neurone in spinal cord, motor neurone, effector — with arrows showing direction of impulse

title: Think About It
content: You tap someone's knee and their leg kicks forward — before they even feel the tap. How is that possible?
bullets: The impulse never reached the brain first
Brain is notified after the response
This is the spinal reflex at work
image: Illustration: doctor tapping patient's knee with reflex hammer — leg extended

title: Spinal Reflexes
content: A spinal reflex is a reflex action in which the impulse is processed by the spinal cord, not the brain.
bullets: Brain is not part of the response
image: Diagram: reflex arc with spinal cord highlighted as the processing centre — brain shown receiving a separate notification signal

title: Spinal Reflex Example: Knee Jerk
content: The knee jerk reflex traces a complete spinal reflex arc — from stimulus at the knee to muscle contraction in the thigh.
image: Step-by-step diagram: knee jerk reflex arc labelled — tap, receptor, sensory neurone, spinal cord, relay neurone, motor neurone, thigh muscle

title: Spinal Reflex Example: Arm Flexing
content: When you touch something painfully hot, your arm pulls back automatically — this is a spinal reflex known as the flexion reflex.
bullets: Stimulus: painful heat or sharp object
Effector: arm muscles flex and withdraw
image: Diagram: hand touching flame — receptor in skin, impulse path to spinal cord, bicep muscle as effector pulling arm back

title: Real Life
content: Spinal reflexes protect you before your brain can react — they are faster than any conscious response you could make.
bullets: The gap explains why you pull back first
Then feel the pain — that's the brain registering
image: Timeline graphic: reflex response (20ms) vs conscious response (200ms) shown side by side

title: Cranial Reflexes
content: A cranial reflex is a reflex action in which the impulse is processed by the brain rather than the spinal cord.
image: Diagram: reflex arc with brain highlighted as the processing centre — showing cranial nerve pathway

title: Cranial Reflex Examples
content: Three key cranial reflexes to know: blinking, coughing, and the pupil reflex — all processed in the brain.
bullets: Pupil reflex: adjusts to light intensity
image: Three small panels: eye blinking, person coughing, pupil constricting in bright light — each labelled with reflex name

title: Conditioned Reflexes
content: A conditioned reflex is a learned response that develops when a neutral stimulus is repeatedly paired with a stimulus that normally triggers a reflex.
bullets: Stimulus becomes a trigger over time
Can be built and also unlearned
Pavlov's experiment is the classic example
image: Illustration: dog, bell, and food dish — showing the association between bell and food

title: Pavlov's Experiment
content: Ivan Pavlov demonstrated conditioned reflexes using dogs — showing that a neutral stimulus (a bell) could trigger salivation after repeated pairing with food.
bullets: Repeated pairing: bell rung before food
image: Three-stage diagram: Stage 1 — food causes salivation; Stage 2 — bell + food together; Stage 3 — bell alone causes salivation

title: Did You Know?
content: You have conditioned reflexes too — your mouth waters when you smell your favourite food cooking, even before you see or taste it.
bullets: The smell is the conditioned stimulus
Your nervous system learned this association
image: Illustration: person smelling nshima cooking from a distance — saliva droplets shown as a humorous exaggeration

title: Summary: Types of Reflex Actions
content: Three types of reflex actions — each defined by where the impulse is processed and how the response is triggered.
bullets: Spinal reflex: processed in spinal cord
image: Comparison table: three columns (Spinal / Cranial / Conditioned) with rows for CNS location, example, and innate/learned
